title: Arriving in Rome
content:
On January 15th I set off on my first international journey. I chose not to partake in the group flight as I was flying out of Boston. I had a layover in Germany, so I went through customs there. Once I was past customs, I found my gate and then explored the airport until it was time to board. I did my best to catch some sleep on the plane, knowing I'd have a full day as soon as I landed. Upon landing in Italy, I collected my bags and communicated with Borromini Institute (the school running the Rome study-abroad program) to meet with the driver to bring me to the apartment in Trastevere I'd be staying in for the duration of the program. Upon arrival to the apartment, I felt the nerves and excitement setting in. Unfortunately, I didn't realize until I arrived that the phone plan I had set up in the States, which was supposed to work in Italy did in fact not work in Italy. I met up with the landlord, who showed me how to work the elevator (quite different than the elevators I was used to) and gave me my key as well as the wifi code (Thank God!!). I settled into the apartment and contacted my professor to let her know I arrived safely. She advised me that her and the rest of the students were at the Borromini Institute and asked if I'd like for her to meet me to walk over. So, data-free phone loaded with directions, I set off on a fifty-minute walk, on my own, in a foreign country to find the school.
Luckily, right outside the apartment, I ran into a couple of girls who were clearly studying abroad as well. Since we were walking in the same direction, I walked with them until we had to part ways. Shortly after which, I ran into my professor and together we made the rest of the journey to the school. Completely overwhelmed by the many sights, sounds, and smells, I was in awe of my surroundings.
Finally, I was in the country of my heritage, the country I had dreamed of visiting ever since I was a child and my mother told me stories of her young childhood in the very city I now stood.

title: First Week in Rome
content:
I navigated my first week in Rome completely data-free. Our first Saturday was spent doing a 'recon' mission with Tom Rankin, one of the instructors at Borromini. He led us throughout Rome, showing us nature and city alike. During this mission, we got to experience all of Rome's public transportation options --- bus, tram, train, and metro.
On Sunday morning, I explored Porta Portese (a large, open air flea market held in Trastevere, right outside my apartment, in fact). decided I wanted to attend Mass at Basilica di Santa Maria a Trastevere, a local Basílica considered to be one of the oldest churches in Rome. As I still didn't have phone service (and my Google maps decided to fail me), I had to ask a woman walking with her kids for directions (in Italian, of course). I made it to the Basilica and enjoyed Mass.
On Tuesday, for our first on-site, out of the classroom excursion for our Monuments and Memories class, we went to the Capitoline Museum and the Roman Forum. It was amazing to see the forum and know that it represented thousands of years of history. But, my favorite part of this day was the original bronze statue of Romulus and Remus. With my family being from Italy, and my mom, uncle, and nonna being specifically from Rome, I had grown up knowing the story and had small images of Lupa around the house. So, seeing the original in person was quite surreal and made me very emotional.
I finally managed to get my phone data sorted out Thursday morning, in perfect time for my weekend adventure.
After arriving in Italy, I learned that some of my extended relatives from home was also in Italy, staying in their house in the village and so I took the train to San Giovanni Incrocio to visit my family.

title: Exploring Bologna and Venice
content:
This week, we had our first trips outside of Rome. We met at the train station early Thursday morning and set off to Bologna. We spent the rest of Thursday exploring Bologna before departing for Venice Friday afternoon. I had some of the best food I've ever had in Bologna! I, unfortunately, had been feeling a bit sick most of the week until this point. So, I decided that my first meal in Bologna would be a simple Tortellini in Brodo, one of the dishes Bologna is known for, and it instantly made me feel better. And for dessert, my friend and I split a piece of Torta Tenerina, a flourless chocolate cake also from the Emilia-Romagna region. And for dinner, I, of course, had to have the classic Pasta Bolognese.
Before heading in for the night on Thursday, my friend and I decided to explore Bologna a bit more on our own to take a look at the architecture and see things we would have a chance to see otherwise. On our adventure, we came across an event skatepark right down the street from the hotel we were staying at. There was an open air market, with people selling clothes and food; people skateboarding, and performers singing, rapping, and even fire-dancing. The people were so awesome, and it was such a cool and authentic experience.
Thursday morning we went to Arte Fiera Bologna, a massive contemporary and modern art fair. There were hundreds of stalls and exhibitions, displaying some of the coolest art I have ever seen. I would have loved to spend the entire day there, but we had a train to Venice to catch. We spent the evening mostly on mainland Venice, where most of the residents live. We had a delectable group dinner at a restaurant on the mainland, including an amazing pumpkin lasagna. After dinner, couple of us decided to take the bus to the island. We walked around at dusk and spent some time at a pretty well-known literary bar. Venice was so calm and peaceful in the evening.
The next morning, we headed back to the island to explore Venice during the day. It was so beautiful and unique, but definitely very tourist-based. We happened to be there for the start of Carnevale, so it was quite busy and filled with people in Carnevale outfits, which was awesome to see. We also got to tour the Venice Biennale headquarters, which super cool to see and compare to ArteFiera Bologna. All in all, it was an amazing expedition.

title: Florence and Catholicism
content:
These past two weeks have been very exciting for me. We spent the past weekend  in Florence and I got to see so many of my favorite pieces of art that I grew up seeing in images online in person - including the statue of David, the Birth of Venus, and Primavera. The food in Florence was also amazing, for lunch I got a traditional Florentine panino and had zuppa inglese (a type of cake from this region, the name of which translates to 'English soup'). I had grown up with my mom making zuppa inglese for pretty much every birthday, so it's something that's very special and meaningful for me. The zuppa inglese I got in Florence Is a lot different than what I'm used to, but it was so delicious nonetheless.
We spent the week learning about Rome's Catholic/Christian history, and the art and architecture that stems from it. As a practicing Catholic who loves studying theology and Church history, this was definitely my favorite unit to date.
We got to go to the Vatican as a group, and had a tour through the Vatican Museum and the Sistine Chapel, and it was absolutely mind-blowingly gorgeous. After the guided tour, a couple of us stayed back to see St. Peter's Basilica as well. There was a mass going on, but I didn't end up staying for it, as it was getting late and the Vatican is pretty far from Trastevere.
This Wednesday was Ash Wednesday, and I had one of the coolest experiences of my life. I was researching Ash Wednesday masses in Rome and came across the mass that Pope Leo XIV was holding at the Basilica of Santa Sabina.
So, as soon as class was over, I raced over to get to the Basilica as soon as possible. Unfortunately, I made it a bit late and hadn't realized that tickets were necessary to get into the Basilica itself, but they had an outdoor area to attend Mass as well, and a big screen to watch the Pontiff. When it was time for the ashes to be distributed, I learned that in Italy, unlike in the United States, ashes are sprinkled on the scalp (in the U.S., they are traditionally smudged on the forehead). And when mass was over, the Pope waved to me from his car.

title: Viterbo: A Day Trip to the City of Popes
content:
This weekend HWS in Rome had a long weekend, so one of the girls and I decided to take a day trip to one of neighboring cities within the Lazio region of Italy. We decided on Viterbo, know as the 'City of Popes', as well as the Pope's spa due to its famous hot springs. Naturally, we decided to visit one of those hot spring resorts.
We met at the train station in Trastevere at 7:30 am to board the 8am train to Viterbo, planning to spend the whole morning and afternoon at the hot springs. We arrived in Viterbo around 9am and tried to head towards the hot springs, only to find that the public transportation was very confusing and sparse. After taking about three wrong buses and missing the bus that only goes to the hot springs once every hour and a half a couple times, we finally made it to the hot springs around 1pm and had a great time. We checked in and hung out at the springs until around 5pm, then headed into the city center for dinner and to explore. At first, we were worried we'd get stuck there, due to the iffy public transit and lack of ubers, but we made the bus back.
We spent the rest of the evening exploring the charming city of Viterbo, a city that looks like the quintessential Italian town. We went to a lovely restaurant and tried some of the local, seasonal dishes. I got a chestnut soup and it was to die for. We wandered into this lovely leather goods shop and I got the cutest faux leather purse.

title: Pigneto: Understanding the Neighborhood
content:
This week was another cool week for me, as I got to explore and learn more about the district in Rome, in which my mother and uncle were born and spent their young childhood years. The apartment that my grandparents had in Rome was located in the neighborhood known as Pigneto.
Unlike the crowded tourist destinations that attract millions of visitors each year, Pigneto offers a glimpse into everyday Roman life. The neighborhood is known for its colorful street art, small local businesses, outdoor cafés, and lively atmosphere. Walking through its streets feels different from walking through the historic center. There are fewer tour groups and souvenir shops, and more residents enjoying their daily routines. As an American student studying abroad, I found myself drawn to this side of Rome because it felt authentic and personal.
As I walked through the neighborhood, I often thought about what life must have been like for them decades ago. The streets have undoubtedly changed over the years. New businesses have opened, buildings have been renovated, and modern influences are visible throughout the area. Yet there were still moments when I could imagine children playing in the streets, families gathering outside, and neighbors who knew one another by name.
One of the things I appreciated most about Pigneto was how it helped me see Rome from a different perspective. Many visitors experience the city through its monuments and historical sites, which are undoubtedly remarkable. However, neighborhoods like Pigneto reveal another side of Rome; one defined not by emperors and ancient architecture, but by ordinary people and their everyday lives. It is a reminder that cities are more than their famous landmarks. They are also collections of personal stories, memories, and communities.
As I spent more time in Pigneto, I began to appreciate the neighborhood not only because of its connection to my family but also because of its character. There is a sense of authenticity that comes from watching daily life unfold naturally. The neighborhood is not trying to impress visitors with grand monuments or famous attractions. Instead, it offers something quieter and, in many ways, more personal. It provides a glimpse into the lives of the people who call it home.
Looking back, Pigneto gave me an opportunity to connect with a part of my family's story in a way that would not have been possible through photographs or conversations alone. Standing in the same neighborhood where my mother and uncle spent their early years created a connection across generations that felt surprisingly powerful. In a city filled with centuries of history, I found myself most drawn to a place that helped me better understand my own. Pigneto reminded me that history is not only found in ancient ruins and famous landmarks. Sometimes it lives in the streets where families grew up, the neighborhoods they called home, and the memories they carry with them long after they leave.
